summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing its most severe surge in residential electric bills in at least two decades, with statewide average bills rising 17.5 percent — or about $28 per month — in the single year ending July 1, 2025, driven by a wave of approved rate hikes from Duke Energy, NIPSCO, and CenterPoint Energy. The Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission (IURC) has launched an unprecedented investigative inquiry into energy affordability, Governor Mike Braun has appointed a new consumer-focused Utility Consumer Counselor, and legislators passed landmark performance-based ratemaking reform (HEA 1002) in 2026, but consumer advocates warn that low-income households remain acutely vulnerable with minimal summer disconnection protections and LIHEAP funding far below demonstrated need. Major pending proceedings — including AES Indiana's contested $192.9 million rate request and Duke Energy's $3.3 billion coal-to-gas conversion — will shape ratepayer costs for years to come while the rapid growth of energy-intensive data centers adds pressure on the grid.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households on fixed incomes
messaging_recommendations.message: You are not alone — Indiana families are paying more for electricity than at any point in the last 20 years, and you have the right to affordable, reliable power. Assistance programs, moratorium protections, and advocacy groups are fighting to lower your bills.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Silverman and Smith emphasize leading with shared identity and concrete stakes; framing the crisis as a collective, structural problem (rather than personal failure) reduces shame, builds solidarity, and motivates engagement with assistance programs and public comment processes.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Indiana state legislators and the Governor's office
messaging_recommendations.message: Record utility bill increases threaten Indiana's working families and economic competitiveness — modernizing oversight through performance-based ratemaking, mandatory low-income discount rates, and summer disconnection bans is the fiscally responsible path forward.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Legislators respond to economic-development and constituent-service frames; pairing affordability with competitiveness and fiscal prudence (principles of strategic credibility and audience alignment in Silverman and Smith) creates bipartisan appeal and counters utility industry framing.

messaging_recommendations.message: The Commission's statutory mandate to ensure 'just and reasonable' rates requires weighing affordability evidence, including disproportionate energy burden on Black, Hispanic, and low-income households, in every rate case decision.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Silverman and Smith highlight the power of legal and normative framing; anchoring advocacy in the IURC's own statutory obligations and data transparency tools (e.g., the Residential Bill Survey) shifts the burden of proof and frames consumer protection as regulatory compliance.

messaging_recommendations.message: Indiana's five monopoly electric utilities raised bills by an average of $28 a month in just one year — money coming directly out of family budgets for groceries, medicine, and rent — while utility shareholders continue to earn guaranteed profits. Hoosiers deserve better.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Silverman and Smith's principle of narrative simplicity dictates using relatable dollar figures and a clear villain/victim structure; vivid, specific numbers ($28/month, $50/month for NIPSCO customers) and the 'monopoly' frame generate media traction and public pressure for regulatory action.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana sought a $491.5 million annual revenue increase in a general base rate case. The IURC approved approximately $295.7 million — roughly 60 percent of the request — to be implemented in two steps, with the first increase (~8%) taking effect in February 2025 and a second (~3%) in early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately +11% overall (~$19.16/month average residential increase); Step 1 effective February 27, 2025; Step 2 effective early 2026

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana sought a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) to retire two coal-fired units at the Cayuga Generating Station and replace them with two new combined-cycle natural gas units at an estimated cost of $3.3 billion, to be financed through a tracker recoverable from ratepayers in six-month increments starting 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Phased rate impacts beginning 2026 through commercial operation of units (projected 2029–2030); estimated $812 million in customer savings over project life per Duke's analysis

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Electric
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed for a $369 million annual electric revenue increase in September 2024; after a settlement reducing the request to approximately $257 million, the IURC approved the settlement on June 26, 2025, with rates phased in beginning July 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately +16.75% for average residential customer (~$23/month); overall statewide residential bills rose approximately 26.7% in the 12-month period after approval

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Natural Gas
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO sought a natural gas base rate increase of approximately $161.9 million annually; a settlement agreement reduced the increase to approximately $120.9 million and was approved by the IURC on July 31, 2024, with rates phased in beginning August 2024 and continuing into Q1 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately +7.1% average (~$5/month) for a residential customer using 72 therms/month

rate_cases.utility: CenterPoint Energy Indiana Electric (formerly Vectren South)
rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy's Indiana electric utility sought an $118.8 million base rate increase serving six southwestern Indiana counties; a partial settlement with industrial customers reduced the increase to $80 million, approved by the IURC on February 3, 2025, with phased bill increases beginning March 2025 and continuing through 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$80 million annual revenue (reduced from $118.8 million); estimated phased impact: ~$10/month (6.5%) in late 2024, additional ~$5.85/month in early 2025, and ~$12.46 in early 2026

rate_cases.utility: AES Indiana (formerly IPL / Indianapolis Power & Light)
rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed for a $192.9 million annual base rate increase on June 3, 2025. A partial settlement filed October 15, 2025 with the City of Indianapolis and industrial customers reduced the requested increase to roughly $10/month for a typical residential customer; the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition opposed the settlement, recommending instead a $21.2 million reduction from current rates. The case remained pending before the IURC as of late 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: Utility initially requested ~$30/month (~13.5%) for 1,000 kWh residential customer; settlement proposes approximately $10/month increase; OUCC recommends a $21.2 million reduction from current rates

rate_cases.utility: Indiana Michigan Power (I&M) — Large Load Tariff
rate_cases.description: Indiana Michigan Power sought and received IURC approval of modifications to its Industrial Power Tariff to accommodate large load customers and data centers, including new cost-allocation protections for residential ratepayers. The settlement was approved on February 19, 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: No direct residential base rate change; tariff modifies cost allocation for large industrial and data center loads

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis of the five investor-owned electric utilities found that Indiana residential bills rose an average of 17.5 percent — or $28 per month — in the 12-month period ending July 1, 2025, the steepest single-year jump in at least two decades. NIPSCO customers were hardest hit at a 26.7 percent increase ($50/month), followed by CenterPoint at 25 percent ($44/month). Consumer advocates called the trend a 'crisis of affordability' and demanded immediate regulatory and legislative action.

articles.headline: Consumer advocates: Spike in electric bills shows Indiana energy plan isn't working
articles.summary: CAC Program Director Ben Inskeep said 2025 marked the highest year-over-year jump in electric bills in two decades, arguing that Indiana's 2023 law requiring the IURC to weigh affordability, reliability, resiliency, stability, and environmental impact is failing ratepayers. Inskeep noted NIPSCO customers were seeing nearly $50-a-month increases, and said the law's affordability provisions lack sufficient enforcement mechanisms. The report was picked up statewide by NPR affiliates WVPE, WBOI, WBAA, and Indiana Public Radio.

articles.headline: 'We can't take it anymore' | Gov. Braun appoints official to fight utility rate hikes in Indiana
articles.summary: Indiana Governor Mike Braun appointed Abby Gray as the new Office of Utility Consumer Counselor commissioner on September 3, 2025, directing her to evaluate utility profits and find cost-saving measures to ease the financial burden on Hoosiers. Braun stated he would like utility investors to bear more of the cost of doing business instead of passing increases entirely to ratepayers. The appointment came as AES Indiana's $192.9 million rate request was pending and electric bills across the state had hit their highest levels in two decades.

articles.headline: Indiana governor seeks lower utility rates: 'We can't take it anymore.'
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun publicly pressed the five largest investor-owned utilities in Indiana — AES, American Electric Power, CenterPoint Energy, Duke Energy, and NiSource — to lower rates, citing the 17.5 percent statewide increase in residential electric bills over the prior year. Braun also directed the newly appointed Utility Consumer Counselor Abby Gray to evaluate utility company profits. The article noted that data center growth in Indiana is adding new demands on the electric grid, complicating affordability efforts.

articles.headline: High utility prices nearing crisis, and there are multiple factors behind the rise
articles.summary: A detailed analysis found that Indiana's rising utility costs stem from multiple compounding factors, including infrastructure investment recovery trackers, rising fuel costs, data center load growth, and insufficient regulatory guardrails. The article noted that so-called 'trackers' allow utilities to adjust rates for specific costs outside the normal rate case process, contributing to frequent bill increases between formal rate cases. Reporters noted the situation had risen to be considered more urgent by some Statehouse observers than the ongoing property tax debate.

articles.headline: State's ratepayer advocate recommends denial of AES' $200M proposed increase
articles.summary: Newly appointed OUCC Commissioner Abby Gray announced that her office found AES Indiana had 'not demonstrated sufficient evidence' for a rate increase and recommended not only denying the $192.9 million request but actually reducing current rates by $21.2 million. Gray highlighted over 6,800 written consumer comments — the most ever received in an individual rate case — reflecting widespread customer hardship. Citizens Action Coalition simultaneously filed testimony urging the IURC to deny the hike in its entirety.

articles.headline: Ratepayer advocates recommend energy bill 'relief plan'
articles.summary: Citizens Action Coalition and Indiana Conservation Voters jointly released a comprehensive ratepayer relief plan in December 2025 calling for low-income discount rates, a summer utility shutoff ban, caps on utility profit rates, and changes to regulatory oversight. The plan proposed tying the low-income discount threshold to a 3–4 percent income burden standard similar to the 30 percent standard used in housing affordability assessments. A Bellwether Research poll cited in the plan found approximately 87 percent of Hoosiers were at least somewhat concerned about rising energy costs.

articles.headline: Duke's $3.3B coal-to-gas conversion, third-party coal study approved
articles.summary: The IURC approved Duke Energy Indiana's $3.3 billion plan to replace two coal-fired units at its aging Cayuga Generating Station with natural gas combined-cycle units, with the costs to be recovered through ratepayer-funded trackers beginning in 2026. Duke estimated the approach would save customers $812 million compared to a traditional rate case but consumer advocates including Citizens Action Coalition strongly opposed it, calling it a 'loss' for both ratepayers and the environment. The approval also included a provision allowing Duke to explore selling the retired coal units to a third party.

articles.headline: AES Indiana electricity disconnections to resume in April 2025
articles.summary: AES Indiana announced it would resume disconnections for nonpayment in mid-April 2025 after pausing shutoffs for more than a year due to widespread billing errors following a system upgrade implemented in late 2023. The billing system issues had affected more than 60,000 customers, generating thousands of complaints to AES and the IURC. The resumption of disconnections came at a moment of heightened concern about affordability as energy bills across Indiana were at record highs.

articles.headline: Indiana to investigate utilities after lawmakers raise concerns over residents' bills
articles.summary: Following a letter from Indiana legislators citing a more-than-90-percent increase in NIPSCO electric bills since 2016, the Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission announced it would launch a formal investigative inquiry into energy affordability. The lawmakers' letter specifically flagged that NIPSCO has among the highest residential consumer rates of all electric utilities reported to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The IURC's inquiry ultimately expanded into a full-day public proceeding held on March 24, 2026.

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: For example, NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana are seeing a nearly $50 a month bill increase this past year. So we're talking really big numbers that we just don't have any precedent for in the last 20 years. Affordability is at a crisis point when it comes to our electric utility bills.
stakeholders.source: WFYI / Indiana Public Broadcasting news report; also WTHR news report

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: Things keeps getting worse for Hoosier consumers and our state leaders continue to allow it to happen. Based on this trend, the rhetoric regarding affordability emanating from the Statehouse appears to be nothing more than lip service. Hoosier consumers can no longer afford monopoly pricing. Something must change, soon.
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition press release 'Monopoly Electric Utilities Slam Hoosiers with Historic Bill Hikes'; also cited in Indiana Capital Chronicle and Fox59

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent, and we hope lawmakers will do their part.
stakeholders.source: Press release on 'Help Hoosiers Now: Ratepayer Relief Plan'; cited in WBIW

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Commissioner (Utility Consumer Counselor)
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help address affordability.
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release, September 10, 2025; cited in Daily Journal and Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Commissioner (Utility Consumer Counselor)
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement.
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release, October 16, 2025; cited in Daily Journal and Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Commissioner (Utility Consumer Counselor)
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: We received thousands of consumer comments in this case, highlighting serious hardships many customers are facing, and I want to thank the individuals who took time to submit comments either in writing or at the IURC's public hearings.
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release, September 10, 2025; cited in Daily Journal

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: I am directing the Utility Consumer Counselor, who is Indiana's ratepayer advocate, to evaluate utility profits and find cost-saving measures to ease the financial burden on Hoosiers. I would also like to see the utilities' investors bear more of the cost of doing business. [paraphrased from combined press statements]
stakeholders.source: WTHR and Utility Dive news reports; Governor's press announcement, September 3, 2025

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: Utility rates are too high. I hear it all the time.
stakeholders.source: 2026 Indiana State of the State Address, cited in WTLC FM and multiple news outlets

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: Energy affordability is one of the most pressing challenges states are facing today. We are asking our five investor-owned utilities that we regulate to come to this room and have an open dialogue about affordability.
stakeholders.source: IURC Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability press conference, cited in Journal Gazette Five Questions interview and AUDACY/WBBM report

stakeholders.name: Sarah Waddle
stakeholders.title: State Director
stakeholders.quote: For older Hoosiers with income constraints, even slight increases in the cost of electricity or heating and cooling their homes can be devastating. That's why we're working hard to lower egregious rate hikes, prevent increases to monthly fees, and educate Hoosiers about their rights.
stakeholders.source: AARP Indiana website article 'Helping Hoosiers Save Money on their Utility Bills'

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: Despite rising costs, we have been disciplined in our planning through measures like our operations and maintenance costs holding flat for the past five years. We have a deep commitment to operating efficiently and keeping rates as low as possible.
stakeholders.source: Statement cited in WISH-TV report on proposed AES Indiana rate settlement, October 2025
